title: Physics & Materials Fundamentals
source_book: Gibilisco, Teach Yourself Electricity & Electronics; Carraher, Giant Molecules

topics.title: Units, Measurement, and Significant Figures
topics.summary: Quality engineering lives on measurement, so the basics matter. SI base units include the meter (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (current), and kelvin (temperature). Every measurement has uncertainty; significant figures communicate how precise a value is, and you never report more precision than your instrument resolves. Accuracy (closeness to the true value) is different from precision (repeatability of readings) — a gauge can be precise but biased (consistently off). Resolution is the smallest increment an instrument can display.
key_points: SI base units: meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin (+ mole, candela).
Accuracy = closeness to true value; Precision = repeatability of readings.
Resolution = smallest increment an instrument can display.
Significant figures convey precision; don't report beyond instrument resolution.

flashcards.a: Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is the repeatability/consistency of repeated readings.
The smallest increment the instrument can display or distinguish.

quiz.q: A scale reads 50.02, 50.01, 50.03 g for a true 49.00 g mass. The scale is:
quiz.explanation: Readings are tightly clustered (precise) but consistently ~1 g high (inaccurate/biased).

topics.title: Force, Stress, and Strain
topics.summary: Mechanical quality work rests on stress and strain. Stress (σ) = force / cross-sectional area (units: pascals or psi). Strain (ε) = change in length / original length (dimensionless). In the elastic region they follow Hooke's law, σ = E·ε, where E is the Young's (elastic) modulus — the material's stiffness. Loading past the yield strength causes permanent (plastic) deformation; the ultimate tensile strength is the peak stress before failure. This is why fastener torque, press fits, and material selection all trace back to stress vs. strength.
key_points: Stress σ = Force / Area; Strain ε = ΔL / L (dimensionless).
Hooke's law (elastic region): σ = E·ε; E = Young's modulus (stiffness).
Yield strength = onset of permanent deformation; UTS = peak stress before failure.

flashcards.a: Stress = force ÷ cross-sectional area; strain = change in length ÷ original length (dimensionless).

flashcards.q: What does Young's modulus (E) represent and how does it relate stress and strain?
flashcards.a: Material stiffness; in the elastic region σ = E·ε (Hooke's law).

flashcards.q: Difference between yield strength and ultimate tensile strength?
flashcards.a: Yield = onset of permanent (plastic) deformation; UTS = maximum stress the material withstands before failure.

quiz.q: A part loaded beyond its yield strength will:
quiz.explanation: Past yield, deformation is plastic (permanent) rather than elastic (recoverable).

quiz.q: Hooke's law in the elastic region states:
quiz.explanation: Stress equals the elastic modulus times strain in the linear-elastic region.

topics.title: Electricity Basics: Voltage, Current, Resistance (Ohm's Law)
topics.summary: Electrical fundamentals underpin EV and sensor quality work. Voltage (V, volts) is the electrical 'pressure' or potential difference; current (I, amperes) is the flow of charge; resistance (R, ohms) opposes that flow. Ohm's law ties them together: V = I·R. Electrical power is P = V·I (watts), which also equals I²·R. Series circuits share the same current and add resistances; parallel circuits share the same voltage and add conductances (reciprocals of resistance). These relations govern wiring, battery packs, and sensor signal integrity.
key_points: Voltage (V) = potential difference; Current (I) = charge flow; Resistance (R) = opposition.
Series: same current, resistances add. Parallel: same voltage, conductances add.

flashcards.a: V = I × R (voltage equals current times resistance).

flashcards.q: Write two forms of the electrical power equation.
In a series circuit, what is shared and what adds up?
flashcards.a: Current is the same throughout; resistances add.

quiz.q: A 12 V source drives 3 A through a resistor. The resistance is:
The power dissipated by that resistor (12 V, 3 A) is:

media.image_caption: Atomic structure — protons, neutrons, and the electrons that carry current
media.web_notes: Current is the flow of electrons through a conductor, voltage is the pressure pushing them, and resistance opposes that flow. Ohm's law ties them together: V = I × R — the single most-used relationship in basic electronics and when reading instrumentation on the floor.

topics.title: Materials & Polymers: Structure Drives Properties
topics.summary: Materials are grouped into metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites, and a material's structure dictates its properties. Polymers ('giant molecules') are long chains of repeating units (monomers); their molecular weight, chain branching, and degree of crystallinity set strength, flexibility, and melting behavior. Thermoplastics soften and can be remelted (reshapeable/recyclable); thermosets cure into a permanent cross-linked network and cannot be remelted. The glass transition temperature (Tg) is where an amorphous polymer shifts from rigid/glassy to soft/rubbery — critical for automotive interior and under-hood parts that must survive temperature extremes.
key_points: Material classes: metals, polymers, ceramics, composites — structure drives properties.
Polymers = long chains of repeating monomers; molecular weight & crystallinity set properties.
Thermoplastics remelt/reshape; thermosets cure permanently (cross-linked) and cannot remelt.
Glass transition temperature (Tg): glassy/rigid below, rubbery/soft above — key for temperature-exposed parts.

flashcards.q: Difference between a thermoplastic and a thermoset?
flashcards.a: Thermoplastics soften and can be remelted/reshaped; thermosets cure into a permanent cross-linked network and cannot be remelted.

flashcards.q: What is the glass transition temperature (Tg)?
flashcards.a: The temperature where an amorphous polymer transitions from a rigid glassy state to a soft rubbery state.

flashcards.q: What molecular factors most influence a polymer's properties?
flashcards.a: Molecular weight, chain branching, and degree of crystallinity.

quiz.q: A material that cures permanently and cannot be remelted is a:
quiz.explanation: Thermosets form a permanent cross-linked network; they char rather than remelt.

quiz.q: Below its Tg, an amorphous polymer is:
quiz.explanation: Below the glass transition temperature the polymer is in a rigid, glassy state.

media.image_caption: Folded, aligned polymer chains form stiff crystalline regions
media.web_notes: A polymer's properties come from how its long chains are arranged. Tightly folded, aligned chains form crystalline regions that are stiff and strong; tangled random coils form amorphous regions that are flexible and tough. The crystalline-to-amorphous ratio is what makes one plastic rigid and another rubbery.
